speaker: interviewer
text: --25, 1996 in Camarillo, California. Mrs. Biniaz, when we left off, you were telling us how you traveled through Germany to come to the United States. But let's back up a little bit. And why don't you tell us what it was like at the DP camp when you got there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there were people from all over who had no way of going back to their countries. And again, we lived in barracks and buildings. Lots of people got married, you know, and some of them actually started on having babies. But we had lived in camp so many years that my parents decided this was not the way to live anymore and started to look on the outside. Went to a place called Mindelheim a small community in Schwabenland. It's on the way to Memmingen. It's between Munich and Memmingen, or Augsburg and Memmingen, the home of the river Mindel. It's very charming, very small. They were able to find [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] an apartment-- a requisition-- a room, really, from a woman whose husband was perished in the war, but was a Nazi. It was-- wasn't really fair, in retrospect, to do this to somebody. I mean, she had nothing to do with it. But that's the way it was after the war. But we lived in her apartment. She was actually the caretaker of the bank, and we lived upstairs. It was an attic apartment. It wasn't very much, really. But it was a better place than living in a camp. And we went on German ration cards, so we didn't even get anything from the camp. We did get packages from UNRRA once in a while, which was wonderful to get that piece of chocolate, and piece of soap.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in the meantime, we had contacted my uncle in the United States, and my uncle in Israel. And they were both working feverishly to send us affidavits to come out. But it took a year and a half for us. But that was my-- that was my return to normalcy, really. And I'm very grateful for having lived for a year and a half in that German town, because it just made me work my way through hate, anger, and all of that. And I left feeling pretty good, you know? When you first arrived there, did you feel a lot of hate and anger?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes, of course. I mean, I had been deprived of my childhood, you know, and really, my teenage years. By the time I came to the United States, I was 16. So in essence, from eight to 16 was gone. But I had a very good experience in Mindelheim. Just by chance, I worked-- I went to a convent. And there was an elderly nun there. She was 92 years old, and I was 14 and a half. And she was going to teach me English and German, because I didn't know any at the time. She had been a cloistered nun. This was a semi-cloistered establishment in the sense that the nuns didn't go out, but the public came in. It was a school for girls.

speaker: interviewee
text: She had entered the convent-- it was called the English Institute. The mother house was in London. She had entered the convent at the age of 16 because of a broken love affair and had never left it. She did not know what it was like on the outside, really. She accepted me totally for what I was, which was wonderful, you know? And she was very kind.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's the only time I cry, when I think about her. She was good, good human being. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I had a good relationship with her for two years-- I mean, year and a half. And then, we corresponded in the United States when I came here. She died at the age of 94. I have her letters. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Those were good Germans. Not everybody was bad. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think living in Germany taught me one thing, that evil can happen anywhere, with any human being, if you give it a chance. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember about coming to the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: We arrived in New York after 11 days on the boat. And we came on a troop ship. We were 60 women in one of the cabins, you know, slept five to a bunk because it was a troop ship. Everybody was seasick except me, so I ate everybody's ice cream and enjoyed it tremendously. We arrived in New York. My uncle came from Des Moines and met us with his brand new car. It was one of the new Cadillacs that just came off the assembly line in 1947. We stayed in New York for a few days. It was exhilarating. New York was very exciting at the time, you know, Broadway, and lights, and the neon lights everywhere. It was just beautiful. And he drove us, then, from New York to Iowa, which was very nice. Get to see part of the country, you know, that way. We didn't stay very long in New York. We stayed about three and a half-- maybe three or four days. That's all. Yeah

speaker: interviewer
text: So when you began your new life in Iowa, what did you do? Did you become a student again? Did you work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right away. As a matter of fact, I-- right away, as soon as we got to Des Moines-- it was in the summer-- I signed up for summer school. I presented my book. I had been tutored in Germany. I you know-- my English nun taught me English and German, but I also was tutored in mathematics and Latin and took violin lessons. And fortunately, I brought all my notebooks with me. And I submitted them to the high school, and they looked them over. And they said that I had covered enough material to enter the 12th grade. However, I did need the requirement of American history, which is an 11th grade subject. So I went into summer school to do American history, to one of the high schools in Des Moines. And then, in September, I entered North High School and graduated in end of May 1948.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your Holocaust experience affect you at all in the way you interacted with other American children when you arrived here?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I'm sure I did. First of all, it was very hard for me to talk about it. Secondly, when I got-- I got-- I was very fortunate to have been able to go to college, to Grinnell College, which is a very nice, private school in Iowa-- a very fine private school. And I think that I was just old beyond my years, you know? I worked very hard. I was on a scholarship, but I had to support myself for board and room by doing all kinds of jobs. And the thing that bothered me most is that the young people who were there who were given the education really didn't appreciate it. But there was a function of the fact that I had gone through things that they hadn't. And it took me about three years to finding-- by my senior year, we were on a par. [LAUGHS] You know, they had matured, and I had leveled off. So I think I enjoyed my senior year the most.

speaker: interviewer
text: But th-- you know, it was difficult for me to accept the fact that they did not appreciate what, what they were given, you know? Were you able to talk about your experiences in the Holocaust to anyone?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really. Not really. I mean, it was-- it's very difficult-- it was very difficult to talk about it, because nobody could comprehend it. And I think that's why I said the film, it sort of opened up the floodgates, because now people who see it are able to ask questions, or at least comprehend one's experience.

speaker: interviewer
text: So after graduating college, what did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I got another fellowship. Well, I spent some-- one summer at Northhampton School of Social Work-- I mean at the Smith School of Social Work in Northampton. And then I was fortunate. I had gotten a fellowship to Columbia University. There were two of us who won $10,000 scholarship for further study out of something like 245 applicants. So that was quite a feather in our cap. And I ended up at Columbia. And I got my master's there in education, and lived at the international house-- met my husband that way.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what's your husband's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Amir. And we got married in 1953. And then I continued. Oh, I had, you know, beyond that-- I went-- you know, after that, we settled, and I continued my education at the schools. And I ended up teaching for about 25 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: At what level?

speaker: interviewee
text: I taught elementary school, and I worked with youngsters who had learning difficulties. I felt that was the right thing to do. I enjoyed it, too, very much. It was very gratifying.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you had a family of your own?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, two children.

speaker: interviewer
text: And their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Robert is my son. And he's married to Lucy and has a little girl named Rachel. She's four years old. And then I have a daughter, Susan. And she's married to Bob. And they have a little boy named Alex, Alexander and another one on the way.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's going to have the baby?

speaker: interviewee
text: Susan.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your daughter.

speaker: interviewee
text: My daughter, in March.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me something. Do you feel that your Holocaust experience influenced you in the way you raised your own children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes it did.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what way?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very much so. I didn't talk to them about it till they-- till my son was 12 and my daughter was 8. They had absolutely no idea that I had been a camp survivor or had-- I didn't want them to have-- I didn't want them to feel guilty. I wanted them to have a happy childhood. I know other people talk about it all the time. And, you know, different people experience it differently. That was my way. I didn't want my children to hate. And I think I achieved that.

speaker: interviewee
text: My daughter has been able to go to Germany many times, and has friends in Germany, has studied in Germany. And has not-- doesn't have the-- didn't go there with, with baggage, you know? She was able to meet people and form her own opinions, not my opinions. And I think, you know-- I think my living in Germany did that for me. I think if I had come straight from camp someplace else, I might have never been able to work through this. But I did work through it, and that was good for me. Did you ever have an occasion to see Oskar Schindler after the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't, but my parents did. They met up with him on a street in Munich just when we were waiting to-- for our shipment-- I mean, to Bremerhaven to go to the United States. And from what I understand, they chatted on the street. And he told them he was particularly happy that we were going to the United States. I think if we had lived in New York area, we might have met up with him. But because my parents lived in Iowa-- and he did come to New York many times. And he did visit friends of my parents, good friends of my parents-- the [? Rosners. ?] And had my parents lived in New York area, they probably would have met up with him. But Iowa was kind of far away.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now that it's over 50 years after the war, is there any message that you would like to leave for future generations for your own children and grandchildren? Ah. Don't hate. Try to live with your neighbor. Accept people for what they are. Everybody has something to offer. Nobody's better than anybody else. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I mean, those are my feelings. Try to see the good in people. Whether they can achieve it, I don't know. But you know, that's, that's the way. I mean, I hate to see what's happening in places like Bosnia and Herzegovina, where you have ethnic cleansing again, the same things over and over again, the hatred that goes on in Israel. I mean, these are all human beings. They all eat the same way, drink the same way, you know? They bleed the same way. So [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] that's my message. Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: We appreciate your taking the time to give us your testimony today. And now we'd like to meet the other members of your family who are here, and look at some of your pictures.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Biniaz, would you please introduce your lovely family to us?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'll be delighted to. This is my mother, Phyllis Karp, who is visiting here from Des Moines, Iowa. She's been here-- she wants me to tell you-- six weeks visiting in Camarillo. And this is my husband, [? Bini. ?] We call him [? Bini ?] for short, but actually his name is Amir, and he lives here. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: I hope so. Mrs. Karp, why don't we start with you. Would you tell me, please, do you think that your experience in the Holocaust influenced you in the way you raised your daughter?

speaker: interviewee
text: You mean after, after the war? Well, not really. We raised her the way, you know-- we thought we did the best, you know? But not influenced by the Holocaust, no. We were very happy and lucky to have her, you know? And we couldn't do enough. I mean, we didn't do so much. She did it all by herself. It's the truth. She was our pride and joy. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I always say, we didn't have the quantity, but we had the quality.

speaker: interviewee
text: Aw. This is embarrassing.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is a wonderful thing.

speaker: interviewee
text: No it is not. It's the truth. Your father was so pr-- he was so proud of her, always talking about her accomplishments, Phi Beta Kappa. I showed off because we had it framed in our second bedroom. And every time he was proud-- if somebody came to visit us, you know, to show how proud, how smart his daughter is.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Bini--

speaker: interviewee
text: That's me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why don't you tell us a few things about your lovely wife. What have you got to add to this testimony?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think my mother-in-law was very eloquent about it. I think Celina is probably one of the smartest ladies I've, I've known-- one of the smartest people I know. And she's a great mother. She's very caring. She's a caregiver. And I'm, just as equally as my mother-in-law, proud to have her in the family.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long have you been married now?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think you'd better help me with that. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: 42 Years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much. We appreciate your taking the time to be with us today.

speaker: interviewee
text: May I add something?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, my husband was sick for awhile, two and a half years. And she was just wonderful-- leaving her husband alone here, and coming to Des Moines to stay to help me out. You know, it's something-- you cannot buy it.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's what every daughter should do.

speaker: interviewee
text: But they don't.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well--

speaker: interviewee
text: You are-- you're exceptional. It's true.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well thank you very much. We appreciate your time. And now we're going to take a look at some of your pictures.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. This is a picture of my mother, Phyllis, and myself as a baby. It was taken in 1931 in Krakow, in Poland. I must be about-- probably five or six months old on that picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you get this picture from?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was probably sent to one of our relatives, either in the United States or in Israel, because we had none of the pictures left. Whatever pictures we see today are pictures that have been preserved by family members, and then given to us after the war. That is a picture of my mother and myself. I am about two years old there-- less probably, a year and a half or two-- on a street in Krakow, Poland, probably taken in 1933.

speaker: interviewer
text: And give us the name of your mother and yourself again, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Celina. And my mother's name in Poland was Felicia, Fela, Felicia. And we went by the name of Karp. That is a picture of me, Celina, on my first day of school, dressed in a uniform. I am six years old, and I'm going to the public school called Adam Mickiewicz, named for the Polish poet. It is taken in the courtyard of our apartment building at 22 [POLISH] in Krakow, Poland. And the year would have to be, then, 1937-- September of 1937. This picture was taken on the ship, SS Marl-- Marlin. And it is taking us from Bremerhaven to the United States. It is my birthday, 28th of May, 1947. On the left hand side is my mother, Phyllis Karp, or at that time, still Felicia Karp. Right next to her is a picture of myself, Celina Karp. I don't know who the other two people are on the right hand side. But looking through the opening on the right hand side, where the people are standing, to the very back there, you see Europe disappearing on the horizon. We were just leaving on the ship from Bremerhaven. This is my concentration camp number, 76318. The KZ stands for Konzentrationslager Auschwitz. Since we were the 300 women who did not get tattooed in Auschwitz, we received these numbers to wear on our uniforms.

speaker: interviewer
text: What does the red triangle stand for? Do you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jew.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's how they designated the Jews, with the red triangle?

speaker: interviewee
text: Either with a star of David, a yellow Star of David, or the red triangle. Actually, the red triangle also meant prisoner, you know? In 1948, I attended the Encampment for Citizenship, which was sponsored by the Ethical Culture Society of New York. The encampment took place at Fieldston School in New York City. Mrs. Roosevelt, Mrs. Eleanor Roosevelt, was a sponsor of the encampment, and we would go to her estate in Hyde Park for a picnic on the lawn. I took this picture of her while she was talking to us with a little Brownie camera. She was a wonderful lady, as you can see that in her face, and in her smile. She was very interested in the young people, and in the experiment in living together of various races and religions. This is a picture of me, Celina. I'm 16 years old. This is 1947, in July-- my first summer in the United States. This picture was taken at my uncle's house in Des Moines, Iowa. I was a teenager. [LAUGHS] In 1993, the film was produced and opened to various-- in various theaters. This is a picture of my mother, Phyllis Karp, my father, Irvin Karp. And the little pot my mother is holding-- the little red pot-- is the pot we received at Schindler's factory from which we drank our hot liquids. My mother has kept it all these years as a reminder of what it was like to be in a camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: The picture was taken in by the people in Des Moines, Iowa, in 1993. This picture was taken in 1989 at Yad Vashem on the Allee of the Righteous in front of Schindler's tree. On the right hand side is my mother, Phyllis Karp. Next to her is my father, Irvin Karp. And I am on the left, Celina Karp Biniaz. OK. This picture was taken at 1582 Calle Aurora in Camarillo, California on December 1994. And the people in the picture are, on the left, myself. Next to me is my son-in-law, Bob Harris, holding up my grandson, Alexander Biniaz Harris. Next to Alexander is my daughter, Susan Biniaz. Below is my husband, Amir Biniaz. Next to him is my granddaughter, Rachel Hood Biniaz Next to her is her mother, Lucy Hood. And next to her is my son, Robert Biniaz We're a happy family. Since I'm a survivor of Schindler's List, I was invited to the preview of the film. My husband took this picture in front of the poster advertising This was the first time I saw the film. I was incredibly moved by it, as was the entire theater. There wasn't a sound emanating from anybody even after the end of the film, except some people were crying. I think the film was exceptionally well done. I think Mr. Spielberg did an excellent job with a very difficult [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] job-- a very difficult topic. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And I think he sort of opened up people's eyes and ears to what was happening at the time, and has made it possible for a lot of people that come forth and speak about their experiences. I know I did not speak about my experiences for over 45 years. And it was only after the film was shown to people that I started talking about it. So I think even for me, Mr. Spielberg made it happen. And I hope he's made it happen for a lot of people. Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much.
